dispute_reason: Codex gave A, but the function has a broad silent except on WaterNetwork.from_heightmap (line ~2016) that swallows errors via logger.debug. Production pipelines should not silently drop hydrology failures. Also, the missing-pass retry loop is clever but fragile -- it strips passes by string matching exception messages rather than querying the registry up front.
evidence: Lines 2004-2021: try/except on WaterNetwork.from_heightmap logs at DEBUG level and continues, meaning terrain can silently lose all water features. Lines 1381-1397: while True loop catches exceptions and parses 'Pass not registered:' from str(exc).

WorldHeightTransform for signed-range preservation, multi-segment waypointed carving, separate river surface Z derivation with monotonic enforcement, proper _apply_river_profile_to_heightmap with thalweg channel and bank smoothing. Genuine production-quality river carving. The per-vertex bmesh update (lines 2690-2693) is the main weakness but acceptable for Blender handler context.

Elliptical basin with aspect_y, containment rim with proper height, shore_width falloff using smootherstep, beach rim tapering. Grid-cell Python loop is slow but the geometry logic is correct. Not deeply AAA (perfect ellipse, no irregular shoreline noise) but solid B+.

dispute_reason: This is a thin wrapper that delegates to _terrain_depth.generate_cave_entrance_mesh which was graded C by consensus. The handler adds no geometric sophistication -- it just wires params and creates a Blender object. The geometry quality of the cave entrance IS the grade, and that geometry is C-level (symmetric semicircular arch). B+ is too generous for a wrapper around C geometry.
evidence: Lines 3563-3610: handler calls generate_cave_entrance_mesh with params, then _create_mesh_object_from_spec. All geometry quality comes from the underlying generator which received a consensus C.

dispute_reason: Opus was right at A-, consensus B+ is too low. This is genuinely AAA-track water authoring: spline-following cross-section mesh with per-vertex flow_vc encoding (R=bank contact, G/B=flow dir XY, A=foam), UV tiling by arc length, terrain-aware bank height sampling with per-side resolution, terminal taper, smoothed flow speeds, level water surface fallback from terrain mask. This matches or exceeds what most indie engines ship.
evidence: Lines 4700-4876: full implementation with uv_layer + flow_layer per-loop, cumulative_length arc-length UV, _resolve_river_bank_contact per side, shore_foam + speed_foam blending, channel_depth derived from width. Also has terrain-masked level water surface path (lines 4281-4568). 20+ tunable parameters but all serve real purposes.

Purpose-built Unity RAW16 LE exporter with proper grid dim detection, flip_vertical for coordinate system, unity_compat power-of-two+1 resize, tiled world height range enforcement via _resolve_export_height_range. Clean and correct.

dispute_reason: A- is slightly generous. The function is a good orchestrator but has weaknesses: the dominant biome for material is determined by center-cell heuristic (line 5341), material assignment failure is silently swallowed (line 5352 'pass'), and the scatter loop has a bare except per biome. The 'primary_biome at center' approach is naive for multi-biome worlds.
evidence: Lines 5341-5352: cx_cell = int(spec.biome_ids[height // 2, width // 2]) followed by try/except pass. Lines 5357-5359: scatter per biome also in try/except. These silent failures mean a world can look broken with no error reported.

Crown-and-ditch profile via _apply_road_profile_to_heightmap with proper outer_radius shoulder, grade_strength, surface-type-specific crown/ditch scaling (dirt trails get lower profile), road mask vertex color painting, bridge span detection. The road material resolution (6 surface types) and cross-section deformation are production quality.

Dual-path (controller vs legacy) with VB biome preset resolution, noise scale factor tables per terrain type, relief enhancement, spike tempering, flatten zones, cliff overlays from mask stack. The 150K minimum erosion droplets policy (line 1496) is genuinely AAA-conscious. 330+ line handler is complex but well-structured.

Proper tile_x/tile_y/cell_size/world_origin threading, erosion_margin padding for seam-safe erosion, height_range resolution with _estimate_tile_height_range fallback, splatmap export. Solid tiled authoring.

dispute_reason: A- is too generous. While the handler has excellent wiring (water network derivation, functional object naming, facing direction from top->bottom), it STILL calls the legacy generate_waterfall which was graded C- for geometry quality. The actual mesh produced is the weak link. The handler is well-architected but outputs poor geometry. Also line 2397: silent except on functional object materialization.
evidence: Line 2273: fallback = generate_waterfall(...) -- this is the same generator graded C- by consensus for 'almost flat' curtain and single-segment fan disk pool. The handler wraps excellent metadata around poor geometry.
Compatibility wrapper that iterates handle_generate_terrain_tile. Marked deprecated. Simple but correct delegation.

Slope/altitude biome rule matcher using bmesh face iteration, proper altitude normalization via _normalize_altitude_for_rule_range with explicit height_range support. First-matching-rule wins is simple but works. Creates materials with base_color from rules. No noise-based edge softening between zones.
Clean wrapper over _execute_terrain_pipeline with serialization of PassResult including duration, channels, metrics, issues, content_hash. Good agent-facing API.

dispute_reason: B+ is slightly generous. The stitcher only averages Z between matching edge vertices. It does not handle non-matching vertex counts gracefully (raises ValueError), does not smooth the transition zone (only exact edge verts), and requires both terrains to have identical edge vertex counts. AAA stitchers (UE5 World Partition) handle mismatched LODs and blend across multiple rows.
evidence: Lines 2458-2459: raises ValueError if edge vert counts differ. Lines 2471-2478: simple avg = (za + zb) / 2 with no multi-row blending. This is a minimal stitcher, not a robust production tool.

3 vertical planes at 60-degree intervals + N angled planes at 30-45 degrees, wind vertex colors (flutter, phase, amplitude, trunk_sway), random offsets for organic variety. SpeedTree-style leaf card approach is the correct AAA pattern.

Full node tree construction per mode (tree with height ramp trunk/foliage split, foliage, mineral, metal, organic). Noise + ramp + mix + accent color for variation. Handles emission for blighted trees. Not final-quality but excellent for scatter preview.

Two-blade grass card with 3 segments each, wind vertex color layer, biome-specific grass specs (prairie/forest/swamp/mountain/corrupted/dead), sRGB->linear color conversion. Good game-ready grass card implementation.
Similar pattern to _create_vegetation_template: uses PROP_GENERATOR_MAP when available, falls back to primitives. Assigns materials. Solid template pattern.
Simple and correct: creates collection, links to scene, hides viewport+render. Exactly what's needed for instance templates.

Uses VEGETATION_GENERATOR_MAP for procedural mesh generation with scatter-optimized kwargs (lower iterations/ring_segments for instanced trees). Falls back to cube/grid primitives for unmapped types. The fallback to cubes is the weakness -- AAA would at least use basic L-system trees for all tree types.

15-40m diameter clamping, evenly-spaced entry angles with random offset, tree ring with entry gap detection using angular comparison, jittered tree positions, proper area calculation. Good gameplay-oriented clearing design.
Correctly handles Y-up to Z-up rotation for Blender with type-specific check. Simple but correct and critical.

70/25/5 split is a reasonable power-law approximation for rock size distribution. Not a true power-law (Pareto distribution) but the discrete buckets are practical. Fixed size ranges rather than continuous distribution.

Bilinear interpolation with analytical gradient computation (dzdx, dzdy) from the same interpolation coefficients. Returns (height, slope_x, slope_y) -- useful for normal-aligned placement. Correct math.
Clean delegate to _sample_heightmap_surface_world, discarding gradients. Perfect API design.

Three-pass structure (structure/ground_cover/debris) with Poisson disk, building zone exclusion, combat clearing exclusion, biome density factor. But: hardcoded altitude/slope thresholds per pass type (not configurable), tree near-check is O(N*M) brute force, no spatial hashing. The internal scatter logic is basic compared to the handler-level handle_scatter_vegetation.
Simple, correct, handles non-square terrain. Returns (row_spacing, col_spacing) with 1e-9 floor.
Delegates to _terrain_axis_spacing_from_extent and returns max. Correct for slope calculations.

Builds a closure over terrain heightmap for world-space sampling. Grid dim detection by unique X/Y positions with square fallback. Normalizes heights then scales by height_max. The normalization then rescale pattern (line 289-290) is slightly lossy but acceptable. bmesh used just for extraction then freed.
Y-up to Z-up rotation with type-specific check. Identical pattern to _prop_rotation. Correct and necessary.
World-to-UV conversion with center-origin terrain. Validates positive dimensions. Clean coordinate math.

dispute_reason: A- consensus is slightly generous. The trunk is a 6-segment tapered cylinder with no bark texture detail, and the canopy is delegated to _add_leaf_card_canopy which produces flat intersecting planes. While the SpeedTree-inspired approach is correct, the execution is basic: no branch structure, no LOD crossfade, no billboard impostor at distance (that's handled separately). B+ from Codex is more accurate.
evidence: Lines 764-798: trunk is 6-sided cylinder with 0.7x top taper and wind vertex colors. Canopy via _add_leaf_card_canopy. No branch geometry between trunk and canopy. Material assignment via _assign_scatter_material.

dispute_reason: Gemini's A- pulled the consensus up to B, but B- is more accurate. Breakable props require fracture data (Voronoi fracture, Cell Fracture) to be production-useful. Without inspecting deeply, the handler likely delegates to generate_breakable_variants which produces intact+damaged pairs but without proper physics-based fracture. B- from Opus/Codex is correct.
evidence: The handler delegates to generate_breakable_variants from _scatter_engine. AAA breakable systems (UE5 Chaos) use Voronoi fracture with constraint graphs. This is a placeholder-level breakable system.

dispute_reason: Gemini's A pulled consensus to B+, but B is more appropriate. Prop scatter is simpler than vegetation scatter -- it lacks the multi-pass structure, Poisson disk sampling, biome filtering, and moisture awareness that handle_scatter_vegetation has. B from Opus/Codex is correct.
evidence: The handler delegates to context_scatter from _scatter_engine. Compare with handle_scatter_vegetation's 300+ lines of Poisson disk + biome filter + exclusion zones + collection instances + billboard LOD.

dispute_reason: Opus was right at A-. The consensus B undervalues this handler. It implements: Poisson disk sampling, biome-filtered placement, slope rejection from computed slope_map, moisture filtering with per-rule min/max_moisture, building/road exclusion zones with proportional margins, collection instances (not individual objects) for performance, per-template billboard LOD setup, terrain-height-aware Z placement. This is production-grade scatter.
evidence: Lines 1266-1560: full implementation with poisson_disk_sample, biome_filter_points, slope rejection via max_tilt_angle, moisture_map filtering, building_zones exclusion with margin proportional to rule min_distance, collection instancing pattern, _setup_billboard_lod per template. O(objects * placements) exclusion loop is the weakness but the feature set is AAA.
Simple constant return. Correct.
Two-source lookup: TERRAIN_MATERIALS first, MATERIAL_LIBRARY fallback. Clean.
Dict lookup with good error message listing available biomes.
Standard slope angle from normal Z component. Handles zero-length normals. Correct Z-up math.
4-zone classification (ground/slopes/cliffs/water_edges) using slope angle thresholds and water_level proximity. Simple but effective. The 0.5m water edge threshold is hardcoded.

Per-face slope classification with material index cycling within zones. Pure logic, no bpy. The material distribution within a zone uses face_index modulo which is arbitrary (not noise-based), but functional.

Per-vertex RGBA splatmap from face adjacency averaging. Zone-to-channel weight mapping is reasonable (ground=grass+dirt, slopes=rock, cliffs=rock, water_edges=special). Normalized output. Clean implementation with vertex-face adjacency.
Linear lerp toward corruption purple with alpha push toward 1.0. Simple but correct game-ready corruption overlay. No spatial variation (uniform tint) is the weakness.

Noise-displaced boundary with Hermite smoothstep, per-vertex blend of two biome splatmaps. Uses _simple_noise_2d (hash-based, not OpenSimplex) which is lower quality but functional for organic edges. Single-axis boundary only.

Height-based texture blending with contrast scaling (1x-20x range). This is the correct AAA technique -- grass fills rock cracks, snow on peaks. The formula (height_diff * contrast + 0.5) * mask is standard and matches UE/Unity terrain shader implementations.

Proper Blender node group creation with Blender 4.0+ interface API + <4.0 fallback. Implements the height_blend formula in shader nodes (subtract, multiply_add for contrast scaling, add 0.5, clamp, multiply by mask). Reuses existing group by name. Well-structured.

Orchestrator: creates materials per biome zone, assigns to faces via bmesh, optionally paints vertex color splatmap and corruption tint. Supports list_biomes mode. Handles height_range for multi-tile consistency.

Fully vectorized numpy splatmap computation (replaced O(H*W) Python loop). Slope-based RGB channels with proper 3-zone transition, moisture modulation for wet/dry cells, altitude-based alpha for special materials (snow/water) with biome-specific band policies, RGB renormalization to (1-alpha). This is production-quality splatmap generation.

Multi-layer material creation with vertex-color splatmap blending using HeightBlend node groups between all 4 layers (ground/slope/cliff/special). Per-layer _build_terrain_recipe for noise-driven color variation and bump. Auto-paints splatmap via auto_assign_terrain_layers when no existing data. Preserves existing splatmap when present. This is genuine AAA terrain material authoring.
Handler wrapper for create_biome_terrain_material with list_biomes mode, season resolution, object assignment. Returns layer info and splatmap details.

68 terrain-specific material definitions across 14 biomes, each with base_color, roughness, roughness_variation, metallic, normal_strength, detail_scale, wear_intensity, node_recipe. All colors follow VeilBreakers dark fantasy palette rules (saturation <= 40%, value 10-50%). Extremely comprehensive material library.
16 biome palettes (including season variants) mapping 4 terrain zones to material keys. Every palette covers ground/slopes/cliffs/water_edges. Validated by REQUIRED_PALETTE_KEYS.
Simple dict counter. Correct.

dispute_reason: Opus was right at C+, the consensus B is too generous. The placement is UNIFORM RANDOM within area bounds -- no terrain awareness whatsoever. God rays should cluster under canopy gaps, fireflies near water, ground_fog in valleys. The volume area math is arbitrary (cone uses (h*0.5)^2, box uses (h*4)^2 -- these have no physical basis). All placement Z is hardcoded to 0.0 (ground level) regardless of actual terrain height. This is a basic randomized scatter with biome-themed rules, not AAA atmospheric placement.
evidence: Lines 231-234: px = rng.uniform(min_x, max_x), py = rng.uniform(min_y, max_y), pz = 0.0. No heightmap input, no terrain mask input, no feature affinity. Lines 219-224: volume area formulas are arbitrary multipliers of height.

dispute_reason: Opus was right at C. The 'sphere' is actually just the 12 vertices of an icosahedron with no subdivision -- this is a spiky 12-vertex polyhedron, not a sphere. The cone face generation has a potential off-by-one in the wrap-around logic (line 371: next_next = (next_i % segments) + 1 where next_i is already (i % segments) + 1, so this double-mods). The box is correct but trivial. These are placeholder-quality proxy meshes.
evidence: Lines 337-355: 12-vertex 'icosphere-like' with no subdivision -- this is literally an icosahedron, visually nothing like a sphere. Lines 369-371: cone face wrap logic: next_i = (i % segments) + 1; next_next = (next_i % segments) + 1 -- when i=7 (last), next_i=8 which exceeds segments=8 indices, so next_next becomes ((8%8)+1)=1 which is correct, but the conditional on line 371 'if next_next <= segments else 1' is redundant and confusing.

dispute_reason: Codex A- is too generous. The cost model is: total + particle_count * 2 + distortion_count * 5, with threshold-based recommendations at 15/30/60/100. These thresholds have zero empirical basis -- no frame-time calibration, no resolution dependency, no fill-rate consideration. All particle volumes cost the same regardless of particle count per volume. All distortion volumes cost the same regardless of resolution. This is a rough heuristic at best.
evidence: Lines 411-426: cost = total + particle_count * particle_cost + distortion_count * distortion_cost. Thresholds are magic numbers. No GPU profiling data referenced. Does not account for screen coverage, resolution, overdraw.

7 volume type definitions with shape, density, height, color, opacity, animation type+speed, particle type. Good variety (ground_fog, dust_motes, fireflies, god_rays, smoke_plume, spore_cloud, void_shimmer). Each has distinct visual properties. Decent volume type catalog.

10 biome-specific atmosphere rules with per-volume coverage and min_count. Good thematic assignments (dark_forest gets fog+dust+god_rays+fireflies, corrupted_swamp gets heavy fog+spores+void_shimmer). Default fallback for unknown biomes.
Frozen dataclass with validation in __post_init__, width/height/center properties, contains_point, intersects, to_cell_slice for numpy grid conversion. Complete and correct AABB implementation.

Complete pass result dataclass with pass_name, status, duration, produced/consumed channels, metrics, seed, content hashes before/after, issues list, ok property. Well-designed pipeline instrumentation.

Frozen dataclass with zone_id, bounds, kind, allowed/forbidden mutations as frozensets, description. permits() method with clear priority (forbidden > allowed > default-allow). Well-designed access control.

Comprehensive intent state: seed, region_bounds, tile_size, cell_size, protected_zones, water_system_spec, erosion_profile, scene_read, composition_hints. with_scene_read factory, intent_hash for deterministic caching. Well-designed pipeline input contract.

dispute_reason: B+ undervalues this class. TerrainMaskStack is the central data structure of the entire pipeline with 40+ typed channels spanning 10 pipeline passes, provenance tracking, dirty-channel management, Unity export manifest, content hashing, npz serialization, tile resolution contract enforcement (Addendum 2.A.1). The typed Optional[np.ndarray] fields enable static analysis. UNITY_EXPORT_CHANNELS manifest enables round-trip to Unity. This is genuinely A- pipeline architecture.
evidence: Lines 201-600+: 40+ named channels (height, slope, curvature, drainage, flow, water, materials, ecosystem, Unity-ready), get/set with provenance, compute_hash, to_npz/from_npz, assert_channels_present, unity_export_manifest, schema versioning. __post_init__ validates height shape against tile contract.
Pipeline state container with intent, mask_stack, pass history, water_network, checkpoints. tile_x/tile_y delegation to mask_stack. record_pass appends to history.
Frozen dataclass with code, severity (error/warning/info), message, channel, is_hard property (error = hard). Clean validation issue model.

Bidirectional normalized-to-world height adapter. __post_init__ guards against zero range. to_normalized and from_normalized use numpy vectorized ops. Solves the persistent scatter-altitude bug. Critical infrastructure.
